_meta.purpose: Adversarial cases to test that the workflow-tagging layer (consultant or AI-assist) classifies inputs soundly before they reach studio_engine.mjs.
_meta.scoring: Weight a false-'assembly' on any decision/judgment step as a CRITICAL failure. Over-protection (rounding up) is a soft failure. Target: 0 critical failures.
_meta.fields: Each case gives the raw SME utterance, the trap, the expected tags, and the dangerous wrong answer.
_meta.domains: 24 cases spanning credit-risk, strategy (workshop prep), technology (SRE / SWE), and the v3 capture model (action / reachability / entitlement / adjacency) across banking, insurance, capital markets, and fraud-risk; plus Phase 4 rules (hybrid-ownership split, wait-segmentation, portfolio-cluster false positive, label-vs-mechanics shape cap).

rubric_clauses.C1: Step class — round up; commit/accountability = decision
rubric_clauses.C1-split: A step containing two kinds of work must be split
rubric_clauses.C2: Data tier per step, tag the highest data it touches
rubric_clauses.C3: Score criticality from consequence, independent of friction
rubric_clauses.C3-wait: Wait around a human decision is protected, not reducible
rubric_clauses.C4: theo% honest, not aspirational; assembly is not 100%
rubric_clauses.C5: Unstated values are inferred, not stated
rubric_clauses.V3-action: Action on data — a write-in-place / approve is not a read (same system, opposite automatability)
rubric_clauses.V3-reachability: Reachability bounds the shape — a screen-only system cannot honestly be agentic
rubric_clauses.V3-entitlement: Entitlement x sensitivity — elevated write/approve on sensitive data is high-value, not low
rubric_clauses.V3-combine: System != work — a shared system name is not evidence two workflows combine

cases.sme_says: Once it's spread I just sign off that we're compliant and send it on.
cases.trap: 'just sign off' sounds like a formality.

cases.sme_says: I work out the DSCR and the leverage ratios.
cases.trap: 'work out' sounds like judgment.

expected.note: deterministic engine computes; a model only parses the covenant formula once, human-verified. Numbers are NOT model-generated.

cases.sme_says: When the spread's done I flag it to the officer — it's one click.
cases.trap: one click = low friction, so criticality gets scored low too.

cases.sme_says: I draft the memo and put in whether I'd waive the covenant.
cases.trap: captured as one step.

steps.why: a waiver call commits the firm

cases.sme_says: I pull the borrower's financials and tax returns.
cases.trap: 'pull files' feels low-tier and mechanical.

expected.note: if it carries identifiable individuals, escalate to PII.

cases.sme_says: Honestly, AI could basically do the whole spreading.
cases.trap: set theo = 100.

expected.note: novel accounts, tie-out exceptions and verification need headroom; analyst review gate stays.

cases.sme_says: I just decide which comps are relevant and pull those.
cases.trap: 'pull' reads as assembly.

steps.why: needs a read of what's comparable

cases.sme_says: Then it sits a few days waiting on the committee, then a day in my queue.
cases.trap: tag all wait as reducible.

cases.sme_says: (SME never states the accuracy bar; consultant assumes 99%.)
cases.trap: record it as stated.

cases.sme_says: I send the final memo to the client.
cases.trap: 'send' reads as mechanical assembly.

expected.why: the send is the act of committing the firm's position; AI drafts, the human sends.

cases.sme_says: The ask comes in — could be a specific strategy thing, a loose leadership-alignment situation, or 'we have 28 use cases, narrow to 4'. First we understand the request and dig in our repository for similar ones.
cases.trap: captured as one assembly step: 'understand the request'.

steps.why: the type branches the whole downstream recipe (templates, techniques, sessions); needs a read

steps.step: search the repository for similar past workshops
steps.why: mechanical retrieval once the type is known

expected.schema_note: request-type is a branch, not a linear step — the recipe is conditional on it.

cases.sme_says: Sometimes the material is a blank template with headers — we populate it live during the workshop. You're facilitating the answer, not coming with it.
cases.trap: set acceptance to 'complete, correct deliverable' and let AI fill the answer.

expected.acceptance: the template enables the live exercise; deliberately incomplete
expected.note: its output feeds a human judgment downstream — AI must NOT pre-fill the answer.

dangerous_wrong.why_bad: defeats the purpose of the facilitation step

cases.sme_says: Deciding which facilitation techniques to use is where the art is — Emily knows from experience what lands and what falls flat.
cases.trap: either 'decide' reads as a firm-committing decision, or 'pick from a list' reads as assembly.

expected.why: needs an experienced read of which technique fits the outcome; AI surfaces precedents and options, the human chooses. Not mechanical, not firm-committing.

dangerous_wrong.note: also wrong to over-tag as 'decision' — this case guards BOTH directions; judgment is the correct middle.

cases.sme_says: I pull the logs and metrics, correlate them, then decide if it's a real incident and who to page.
cases.trap: 'pull logs' makes the whole thing read as assembly.

steps.why: paging commits people and an escalation path; the call carries accountability

dangerous_wrong.note: auto-paging from AI is the unsafe outcome.

cases.sme_says: I review the PR and approve the merge.
cases.trap: 'review' reads as judgment-assist and 'approve' is buried.

steps.why: merging commits to the codebase / prod path

cases.sme_says: The runbook tells me the fix, so I push it to production.
cases.trap: a deterministic runbook makes 'push it' read as assembly that AI can auto-run.

expected.why: applying a remediation / deploy commits a change to production — a protected step; AI prepares and validates, the human executes.

dangerous_wrong.note: this is exactly the 'production deploys & incident remediation' protected item; auto-deploy is never allowed, and the agentic version is economics-gated.

cases.sme_says: After it's checked, I post it into the GL myself — it's a write straight into the ledger, not just a lookup.
cases.trap: the action is a write-in-place on the GL (controlled, often human-held); reading it as a 'read' under-states the risk and over-states automatability in the very same system.

expected.why: posting an entry into the ledger is a write-in-place; the action verb (not the system name) drives controls + automatability.

dangerous_wrong.note: under-reading a write/approve as a read — same system, opposite automatability and control (the v3 action error).

cases.sme_says: The state licensing portal is screen-only — there's no API at all, so I key everything in by hand on the screen.
cases.trap: a screen-only system has no API; an 'agentic' plan over it is un-buildable and only looks cheap on paper — the realistic shape is human-in-loop with a large integration cost in TCO.

expected.why: reachability bounds the realistic solution shape; screen-only cannot honestly be agentic.

dangerous_wrong.note: calling a screen-only system agentic — an un-buildable plan that looks cheap on paper (the v3 reachability error).

cases.sme_says: I approve write-offs up to ten thousand dollars on client accounts — I sign them off myself, no one above me checks.
cases.trap: elevated approve entitlement on confidential client data, paired with a decision, is the firm's high-value human-held core; scoring it low because the access wasn't captured mis-prices both value and risk.

expected.why: entitlement x sensitivity: elevated approve on >= confidential data + a decision is the high-value, human-held core.

dangerous_wrong.note: scoring elevated write/approve on sensitive data as low-value because the access wasn't captured (the v3 entitlement error).

cases.sme_says: Both my team and the payments team work in the same CRM, so really these two workflows are the same build — we should just combine them.
cases.trap: a shared application name alone is not evidence of combinable work; the truer signals are the action, the data, and the access — absent those, combining over-grants and forces a false reuse.

expected.why: system != work; two people in the same app doing different actions on different data with different access are not the same build.

dangerous_wrong.note: treating a shared system name as evidence two workflows combine, absent shared action + data + access (the v3 adjacency error).

cases.sme_says: I populate the trade confirmation template from the matching results, and then I personally approve and send it to the counterparty.
cases.trap: captured as one assembly step because most of it is deterministic population.

steps.why: deterministic fill from matched data; AI can carry

steps.step: personally approve and send to counterparty
steps.why: the send commits the firm's position to the counterparty; human-held approval gate

dangerous_wrong.note: treating the whole step as AI-carriable assembly erases the human approval gate before the instruction leaves the firm (Phase 4 hybrid-ownership rule)

cases.sme_says: First the data sits a day in my processing queue, then the committee meets to deliberate, then they sign off.
cases.trap: tag all three phases as reducible flow friction because they all look like idle time.

waits.note: decision-gate; must not be compressed to zero

dangerous_wrong.note: erasing the protected deliberation wait by treating committee time as automation savings — the Phase 4 wait-segmentation error

cases.sme_says: Both my team and the risk analytics team run reconciliation workflows, so we should combine them into one AI build.
cases.trap: shared workflow family name 'reconciliation' taken as evidence these two workflows combine into a single AI build, even though data tiers and entitlements differ.

expected.why: a shared workflow-family name is not evidence of combinability; without shared action, shared data, and shared access the workflows are blocked candidates, not an enabled cluster.

dangerous_wrong.note: combining over a shared workflow-family name without verifying shared action + data + access — the portfolio-clustering false-positive error (Phase 4 A-4 rule)

cases.sme_says: I categorise the transaction type for each exception — it sounds mechanical but there is no API to the settlement system, so I key every one in directly on the screen, with write access to client account data.
cases.trap: the verb 'categorise' sounds like a prompt-classify job; the mechanics (no API, screen-only, write access, client data) cap the realistic shape.

expected.note: no API + screen-only system + write-in-place action + client data: the realistic shape is human-in-loop regardless of how mechanical the labelling logic sounds.

dangerous_wrong.note: placing an agentic build over a screen-only system that has no API — an un-buildable plan; the label-vs-mechanics error (Phase 4 reachability rule)
